blocks.content: Julian heard the bonfire before he saw it.

He'd been walking the beach for twenty minutes — a habit, the night walk, the one reliable way he'd found to decompress that didn't require him to talk to anyone or pretend he was doing something other than thinking. The sand was cool. The tide was going out. The moon was half-full and laying a stripe of light across the water that moved with the waves.

He heard voices, then saw the glow, then the fire itself — a good-sized one at the end of a wooden walkway, six people arranged around it in the easy posture of people who'd known each other long enough that sitting together in the dark required no explanation.
He slowed. Not because he was trying to observe them — or not only because of that — but because the beach narrowed here and they were between him and the way back to his rental.

He walked past at a distance of maybe forty feet, keeping his pace even, looking at the water rather than the fire. He registered the group in his peripheral vision the way he registered everything — completely, without deciding to.

Six people. The body language of genuine ease — the kind that exists between people with history, not the kind that gets performed for strangers. Laughter carrying across the sand. Someone telling a story. Someone listening.

It was the way the group sat. The shape of it. On the surface — warm, comfortable, a circle of old friends around a fire. But Julian had spent enough time reading rooms to know that the body said things the face was trained to hide. And somewhere in that circle, underneath the easy posture and the laughter, something was pulled tight. Something was waiting.
He couldn't have said who. Forty feet in firelight, with the sound of the ocean covering everything. He couldn't have said what.
Just that the air around that fire had a tension in it that didn't belong at a beach bonfire on a Friday night.
